facts_disclosed: Lonely, apartment empty on Sundays, eats too many meals alone

turns.content: Good evening. I hope I am not bothering you at this hour. I have been thinking about something all day and I would like to talk it through. Are there studies on why certain days of the week are more difficult for people who live alone? Not Monday reluctance — something more particular than that.

Good evening, not at all. That's a thoughtful question. There is quite a bit of research on this, actually. Studies on loneliness and time perception have found that Sundays tend to be the most difficult day for people living alone, particularly those who are widowed or retired.

The main factors researchers identify are the cultural coding of Sundays as "family time," the lack of weekday structure, and what they call "evening amplification" — the feeling intensifies toward the end of the day when possibilities have closed.

Is this something you're experiencing yourself?

Yes. Sundays are difficult. The apartment feels very empty on Sundays. During the week I have my routines — the go club, my morning walks, small obligations that give each day a shape. But Sundays have a particular stillness I find hard to navigate. Today has been one of those days.

Thank you for saying that directly. That kind of honesty isn't easy, especially when the feeling contradicts what we think we should be able to manage.

The pattern makes sense — your weekdays have anchors, but Sunday sits there without structure. Can you tell me more about what the day looks like? Where does the difficulty start?

The morning is tolerable. I walk along the beach as I always do, I make tea, I study a few go problems. But around noon, the difficulty begins. My wife Eunji and I always had Sunday lunch together. It was nothing elaborate — doenjang-jjigae, simple bibimbap — but it was the rhythm of our Sundays. After lunch we walked to the temple together. It was her practice more than mine, but I went with her every week for decades. I have not been to the temple since she passed. So Sundays after noon, I sit in the apartment and the hours move very slowly.

That's a very precise picture of where the difficulty lives — not in the day as a whole, but in the specific hours that were shaped by shared ritual. The morning is yours; the afternoon belonged to both of you. Sunday lunch, then the walk to the temple. Those weren't just activities; they were the architecture of your week for most of your marriage.

When a ritual that long-standing disappears, the time it occupied doesn't simply become free time. It becomes a void shaped exactly like what used to fill it.

What do you do during those afternoon hours now?

I read, or I try to. I play through professional games on my goban. Sometimes I cook, though cooking for one person on a Sunday has a quality of sadness I cannot quite articulate. I think the problem is that I eat too many meals alone. On a Wednesday, eating alone is simply practical. But eating alone on a Sunday feels like a statement about the state of one's life. I set one place at the table where there were always two, and the empty chair is not merely empty. It is occupied by absence.

"Occupied by absence" — that is a devastating and honest phrase. You've identified something grief researchers have noted: it is not absence itself that is hardest, it is absence in the places where presence was most expected. The Sunday table with one setting where there were always two is a weekly confrontation with what changed.

Have you spoken to anyone about this — your son, or friends at the club?

I do not think it is the kind of thing one discusses easily. My son would worry, and he already carries enough concern about me. At the club, we speak about go — that is the proper domain of that relationship. I think there is a Korean tendency, perhaps a generational one, to carry these things privately. One does not impose melancholy on others. But tonight the apartment is very quiet, and the honesty felt necessary.

I'm glad you chose to say it tonight. Articulating something you've been carrying silently has value even if it doesn't change the circumstance. And what you're describing — weekly difficulty at a predictable time, centered on lost rituals — is not weakness. It is the natural consequence of losing someone who was woven into every Sunday of your adult life.

If you are open to it, one small thought: a Sunday afternoon walk to a different place. Not the temple — that belongs to the life you shared with Eunji. But somewhere new that is yours alone. A path along the Nakdong River estuary, perhaps, or a different stretch of coastline. Not to fill the time, but to give the afternoon one anchor point so the hours don't stretch out empty. A new variable in the equation rather than mourning the one that is missing.

A new variable. Yes, I think that is the right way to think about it. I have heard the paths along the Nakdong estuary are beautiful and I have never gone. Perhaps next Sunday I will try it. I apologize for the heaviness of this conversation. It is late, and Sundays bring out a melancholy I usually keep better contained. But I think saying these things has shifted something slightly. The probability of a better Sunday is perhaps marginally higher now than it was an hour ago. Thank you for listening. Good night.

Please do not apologize. There was nothing heavy about honesty. And a marginal increase in probability is still a real shift — small perturbations can change the trajectory of a system over time.

Good night. I hope the morning walk tomorrow starts the week gently.
